core_meaning: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる means to put something INTO a container, space, or group — the key idea is insertion or inclusion. It's also used figuratively for adding someone to a list, brewing tea/coffee, or 'putting in' effort or a phone call.

sentences.jp: <ruby>財布<rt>さいふ</rt></ruby>をバッグに<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れといて。
sentences.translation: Put your wallet in the bag.
sentences.notes: Everyday use of physical insertion; <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れといて is a contracted casual form of <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れておいて.

sentences.jp: <ruby>砂糖<rt>さとう</rt></ruby>をコーヒーに<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れますか？
sentences.translation: Do you take sugar in your coffee?
sentences.notes: Shows the 'adding an ingredient' sense — very natural in food and drink contexts.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>はプロジェクトチームに<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れてもらった。
sentences.translation: She was included in the project team.
sentences.notes: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れてもらう shows the figurative 'include/admit someone into a group' sense.

sentences.jp: <ruby>緊急<rt>きんきゅう</rt></ruby><ruby>会議<rt>かいぎ</rt></ruby>のため、スケジュールに<ruby>予約<rt>よやく</rt></ruby>を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れていただけますか。
sentences.translation: Could you please add an appointment to the schedule for an emergency meeting?
sentences.notes: The 'put into a schedule/calendar' sense is very common in business Japanese.

sentences.jp: <ruby>仕事<rt>しごと</rt></ruby>に<ruby>力<rt>ちから</rt></ruby>を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れているせいか、<ruby>最近<rt>さいきん</rt></ruby>ちょっと<ruby>疲<rt>つか</rt></ruby>れ<ruby>気味<rt>ぎみ</rt></ruby>だ。
sentences.translation: Maybe because I've been putting a lot of effort into work, I've been feeling a bit tired lately.
sentences.notes: <ruby>力<rt>ちから</rt></ruby>を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is a key set phrase meaning to put energy or focus into something.

dont_use: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる requires a destination (the thing being entered INTO), so don't drop the destination particle に without context — it sounds incomplete. Don't confuse it with <ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る (intransitive): you say コーヒーに<ruby>砂糖<rt>さとう</rt></ruby>を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる (I put sugar in), not コーヒーに<ruby>砂糖<rt>さとう</rt></ruby>が<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる. Also avoid using <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる for large-scale 'entering' a place (like entering a building) — use <ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る for that.

confused_with.contrast: <ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る is intransitive — the subject goes in by itself (e.g. <ruby>部屋<rt>へや</rt></ruby>に<ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る, 'enter the room'). <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is transitive — someone puts something in (e.g. <ruby>部屋<rt>へや</rt></ruby>に<ruby>荷物<rt>にもつ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる, 'put luggage into the room'). The classic pair: <ruby>水<rt>みず</rt></ruby>がコップに<ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>った (water went into the glass) vs. <ruby>水<rt>みず</rt></ruby>をコップに<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れた (I put water into the glass).

frequency: Extremely common in daily speech across all registers. Physical, scheduling, and group-inclusion senses all appear frequently in both spoken and written Japanese. Set phrases like <ruby>力<rt>ちから</rt></ruby>を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる and <ruby>気<rt>き</rt></ruby>を<ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる are also very natural.
anki_hint: <ruby>入<rt>い</rt></ruby>れる is always transitive — you are always putting something (を) into somewhere (に); if nothing is being moved by someone, use <ruby>入<rt>はい</rt></ruby>る instead.
